Projects

Work

01 — Open Source

miniROV

Active Development

Tethered underwater ROV with LLM-assisted supervised autonomy for UK coastal and lake deployments. Natural language commands → Ollama (qwen2.5:14b) → structured JSON → pymavlink → ArduSub. Manual override retained at all times.

ArduSub MAVLink / pymavlink Ollama · qwen2.5:14b Raspberry Pi 5 Navigator FC 4× T200 Thrusters Python SITL

System Architecture

Two-node topology over a 50 m tether. Topside: Apple M4 Pro running Ollama locally and a Python minirov-brain agent for command arbitration and safety envelopes. Subsea: Raspberry Pi 5 running ArduSub via Blue Robotics Navigator Hat, driving 4× T200 thrusters. Zero cloud dependency in the inference loop.


NL → MAVLink Pipeline

01
Natural Language Input
Plain-English operator commands — e.g. "descend to 3 m, hold north."
02
Ollama Inference (qwen2.5:14b)
Local LLM parses intent, returns structured JSON action payload. No external API.
03
minirov-brain Arbitration
Validates JSON schema, enforces safety envelopes, converts to MAVLink RC override via pymavlink.
04
ArduSub Execution
RC overrides reach ArduSub Stable-4.1 — stabilisation, depth hold, thruster mixing. Manual override always available.

Specifications

Topside ComputeApple M4 Pro (Ollama local)
Subsea ComputeRaspberry Pi 5
Flight ControllerBlue Robotics Navigator Hat
FirmwareArduSub Stable-4.1
Thrusters4× Blue Robotics T200
Tether50 m (comms + power)
LLMqwen2.5:14b via Ollama
Interfacepymavlink RC override
SimulationArduSub SITL
TargetUK coastal & lake

Development Phases

Phase 0 — Software foundations & SITL ✓
Phase 1 — Electronics bench build
Phase 2 — Enclosure & frame assembly
Phase 3 — Pool and tank testing
Phase 4 — UK coastal field deployment

02 — MSc Thesis

Underwater
Glider

Complete

Fully deployable buoyancy-driven glider platform fitted with a NaI nuclear scintillator for oceanic radiation mapping. Designed end-to-end in OnShape — watertight acrylic enclosure, NEMA-17 leadscrew buoyancy engine, centre-of-mass shifter — fabricated, assembled, and tested in a small water enclosure.

Buoyancy Engine NaI Scintillator Hydrodynamics OnShape CAD SLA / FDM Print Arduino Radiation Mapping

Design Rationale

Gliders offer far greater endurance-to-cost than propeller-driven AUVs. Gamma rays attenuate rapidly in seawater — peak contamination concentrations occur in the upper water column, making a shallow-survey glider the right platform for radiation mapping. The design minimised acoustic signature to protect scintillator SNR, and prioritised deployability without specialist equipment.

System Overview

EnclosureCast acrylic tube, 4″ (Ø114.3 mm)
End capsAluminium 6061-T6, Type III anodised
HydrofoilsLaser-cut acrylic, Al + SLA mounts
ElectronicsArduino Uno + stepper motor drivers
Power12 V LiPo 550 mAh
PayloadNaI scintillator + NanoPi
CADOnShape — full assembly drawings

Buoyancy Engine

A NEMA-17 stepper + T8 stainless leadscrew drives a BD Plastipak syringe plunger, modulating displaced volume to control buoyancy. SLA-printed support plates constrain the syringe body on 6 mm carbon fibre rails. The Arduino control loop reads a depth/pressure target, steps the motor to the required syringe position, and holds. Feedback integration with a pressure sensor is a planned next step.

Buoyancy engine bench test — Arduino Uno, NI ELVIS III, dual-syringe prototype

Full assembly — buoyancy engine (left) and CoM shifter (right) both visible

Centre-of-Mass Shifter

A second NEMA-17 + leadscrew mechanism translates the battery pack longitudinally within the hull, trimming pitch attitude. This eliminates the need for external control surfaces and their associated drag. By commanding both leadscrews — buoyancy for depth rate, CoM for pitch angle — the glider executes a sawtooth dive/climb trajectory, converting vertical motion to forward glide via the fixed hydrofoils.

Sensing Payload & Water Enclosure Test

The payload integrates a NaI scintillator for in-situ gamma detection, feeding a NanoPi onboard computer. Measurements are timestamped against GPS fix and pressure-derived depth, producing depth-resolved radiation maps. Buoyancy and sealing were validated in a small water enclosure — the hull held pressure and the buoyancy engine cycled correctly through dive and ascent commands.

Water enclosure test — buoyancy validation, no hydrofoils fitted

Lessons & Next Steps

Flat-plate wings to be replaced with a symmetrical thin hydrofoil planform for improved glide ratio. Syringe/stepper buoyancy engine to be replaced with a compact higher-force actuator. Pressure sensor + IMU required to close the depth/pitch feedback loop properly. Roll control mechanism to be designed and added.

03 — Team of Five

Tricopter
UAV

Complete

Custom tricopter UAV with servo-actuated thrust vectoring yaw — eliminating the counter-rotor — and an in-house electromagnetic pickup/release mechanism for 150 g vaccine payload delivery at 15-minute loaded endurance.

Thrust Vector Yaw Servo Actuation Carbon Fibre Frame 15 min Endurance 150 g Payload EM Release

Top-down bench view — FC, ESCs, vectoring servo mount visible

Yaw Control Architecture

The aft rotor is mounted on a servo-driven vectoring mount, rotating ±30° to generate yaw torque — no counter-rotating propeller pair required. This avoids the weight and drag penalty of a fourth motor while retaining payload-fraction efficiency. The mount was tuned to minimise cross-coupling with roll and pitch channels.


Design Challenges

The vectoring mount introduces PID tuning complexity — aft rotor thrust vector rotation affects pitch authority at servo travel extremes, requiring mechanical travel limits and gain scheduling to prevent attitude excursions under payload. Endurance vs. payload fraction drove motor and propeller selection throughout.

Payload Delivery Mechanism

In-house latched electromagnet release activated via flight controller auxiliary channel. Sized for a standard vaccine cold-chain pack (150 g max). The latch mechanism was designed for field reliability under continuous vibration loading from all three rotors, with a positive retention force sufficient to prevent premature release in turbulence.
